findings.title: processJobInner parameter types `any[]` for platforms
findings.detail: The `processJobInner` function signature is typed as `job: Awaited<ReturnType<typeof db.distributionJob.findUnique>> & { platforms: any[] }`. This means every property access on elements of `job.platforms` — `p.platform`, `p.status`, `p.id`, `p.externalUrl`, `p.externalId` — is unchecked. Any rename or schema change in the Prisma model silently passes the compiler. The `any[]` should be replaced with the Prisma-generated type for `distributionJobPlatform`, which is already available as `Prisma.DistributionJobPlatform` or inferred from the `include: { platforms: true }` query shape.

findings.title: `meta as any` passed to Prisma update after mutating the cast object in-place
findings.detail: The pattern on lines 96–104 is: fetch the job, cast `job?.metadata` to `Record<string, unknown>`, mutate the object in-place (`meta.verification = { ... }`), then pass `meta as any` to Prisma. Two problems. First, the `as any` is used specifically because `Record<string, unknown>` is not assignable to Prisma's `InputJsonValue` — the correct fix is to declare the spread as `Prisma.JsonObject` or use `satisfies Prisma.InputJsonValue`, not silence the error with `any`. Second, mutating a cast object that holds a direct reference to the Prisma-returned `JsonValue` creates a subtle read-modify-write race: if two calls run concurrently on the same job (e.g. a retry), the second read can be stale, and the in-place mutation of the first result is meaningless. The correct pattern is to spread into a new object: `const updatedMeta = { ...meta, verification: { ... } }` and pass `updatedMeta` typed correctly.

findings.title: Repeated `metadata as Record<string, unknown>` + property-by-property unsafe casts throughout processor
findings.detail: The `job.metadata` field (Prisma `Json`, TypeScript `JsonValue`) is cast to `Record<string, unknown>` at least four separate times in `processJobInner` (`metadata`, `currentMetadata`, `updatedMetadata`, `currentMeta`). Each cast is followed by string casts like `metadata.description as string`, `metadata.tags as string[]`, `metadata.isDraft as boolean`, etc. None of these are narrowed — if the DB value is missing or the wrong shape the cast silently produces `undefined` at runtime without any type error. The correct approach is to parse and validate the metadata object once at the top of `processJobInner` using a narrow schema (a small hand-written parser or `zod.parse`) and work with a typed `ParsedMetadata` throughout. This would collapse the repeated casts and make every access safe.

findings.title: `metadata as Record<string, unknown>` in `updateDistribution` then spread without narrowing
findings.detail: In `updateDistribution`, `job.metadata` is cast to `Record<string, unknown>` and spread directly into the Prisma update payload (`{ ...existingMetadata, description: data.description, ... }`). Because `job.metadata` is `Prisma.JsonValue` (which includes `string | number | boolean | null | JsonArray | JsonObject`), the cast compiles but is unsound — if the stored value is a primitive JSON type (e.g. `null` due to a bug), the spread `{ ...null }` silently produces an empty object. A guard of `typeof existingMetadata === 'object' && existingMetadata !== null` before the spread would make this safe.

findings.title: `extractYoutubeVideoId` result used with `?? ""` fallback instead of being guarded
findings.detail: On lines 91–92, `extractYoutubeVideoId(youtubeUrl)` is called twice and the `null` case is silenced with `?? ""`. Because `youtubeUrl` is already a required parameter typed as `string` (not `string | null`), callers of `publishToWordPress` should have validated it. However, a caller passing an invalid URL would write an empty string `""` into both the `youtube_video_id` and `youtube_thumbnail_url` meta fields without any error or log. The function should either assert the ID is non-null (since the URL was already validated upstream), or extract once, throw if null, and use the variable for both fields. As written, a bad URL produces silent garbage in the WP post metadata.

findings.title: FormState return type is misleading when redirect() throws
findings.detail: `submitDistribution` is declared as `Promise<FormState>` but calls `redirect('/login')` and `redirect('/dashboard')` on the auth failure paths. In Next.js, `redirect()` throws a special `NEXT_REDIRECT` error — it never returns. The return type `Promise<FormState>` implies the function always resolves, which is technically inaccurate and can confuse readers about control flow. This is a documentation/clarity issue rather than a runtime bug (it matches the `useActionState` contract), but annotating or commenting those paths would help future readers. Low severity because it is a Next.js convention issue, not a type hole.

findings.title: `Readable.fromWeb(response.body as any)` repeated — `as any` hides a Node/Web stream incompatibility
findings.detail: The `response.body as any` cast appears twice in `processJobInner` to work around the TypeScript mismatch between `ReadableStream<Uint8Array>` (Web Streams API) and `NodeJS.ReadableStream` (what `pipeline` expects). The same cast also appears in `audio-extractor.ts`. The correct typed fix is `Readable.fromWeb(response.body as ReadableStream<Uint8Array>)` — the cast to `ReadableStream<Uint8Array>` is sound because `fetch` guarantees that type, while `as any` silences all checking. This is low severity (the runtime behavior is correct) but the `any` casts accumulate across files and erode the "no `any`" baseline.

residual_risks.detail: The job timeout (30 min) uses Promise.race with a setTimeout but the inner async work (yt-dlp download, YouTube upload, Transistor upload) is not cancelled — it keeps running in the background after the timeout rejects. Node.js will not GC those promises until they settle. For long jobs this can cause resource leaks (open file handles, in-progress HTTP streams). This is an operational risk rather than a type issue, but it should be tracked.

Verification runs `Promise.all(checks)` where each check pushes into a shared `issues` array. Because JavaScript is single-threaded this is safe, but if the pattern is extended to actual parallelism (e.g., workers), the shared array becomes a race. Currently fine, low future risk.

`updatedMetadata.thumbnailGcsPath = gcsPath` mutates the in-memory object that was cast from `job.metadata`. This mutation is then persisted via a separate DB update. If that DB write fails (line 459), the in-memory mutation persists for the remainder of `processJobInner` — Transistor and WordPress both read `updatedMetadata.thumbnailGcsPath` and will use the value that was never committed to DB. On retry, the job would re-fetch from DB and not have the thumbnail GCS path, causing it to be re-fetched from YouTube. This is a correctness risk on partial failures.

testing_gaps.detail: No tests visible for the YouTube live recording path (`existingYoutubeUrl` present, `gcsPath` absent). This new branch in `processJobInner` skips audio extraction and goes directly to `downloadYouTubeVideoToGcs`. The path through `youtubeAudioPath` assignment and the conditional `!existingYoutubeUrl` check on the video download (line 248) is untested — a test that exercises both `existingYoutubeUrl` set and not set would catch regressions in that routing logic.

The `verifyDistribution` function performs live API calls (YouTube Data API, Transistor API, WP REST API). There are no stubs or mocks visible, meaning it cannot be unit tested in CI without live credentials. The three `verifyYouTube`, `verifyTransistor`, `verifyWordPress` functions are private — they should be extracted and exported for isolated testing.

`updateDistribution` in actions.ts has no tests for the case where `data.platforms` contains values that are not in `VALID_PLATFORMS`. The filter on line 257 silently drops invalid platforms — a test would confirm the guard is working and that the `deleteMany` + `createMany` transaction correctly replaces the platform list.
